revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23989927232|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ar:هيروشيغه]][[arz:هيروشيجه]][[ast:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[avk:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[ba:Утагава Хиросигэ]][[be:Анда Хіросігэ]][[bg:Андо Хирошиге]][[ca:Andō Hiroshige]][[ckb:ھیرۆشیگێ]][[cs:Hirošige]][[cv:Утагава Хиросигэ]][[cy:Hiroshige]][[de:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[el:Χιρόσιγκε]][[en:Hiroshige]][[eo:Ando Hiroŝige]][[es:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[et:Hiroshige]][[eu:Hiroshige]][[fa:هیروشیگه]][[fi:Hiroshige]][[fr:Hiroshige]][[fy:Hiroshige Ando]][[gl:Hiroshige]][[he:הירושיגה]][[hi:एंदो हिरोशिघे]][[hr:Hiroshige]][[ht:Hiroshige]][[hu:Utagava Hirosige]][[hy:Ուտագավա Հիրոշիգե]][[id:Hiroshige]][[it:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[ja:歌川広重]][[jv:Hiroshige]][[ka:ანდო ჰიროშიგე]][[kk:Утагава Хиросигэ]][[ko:우타가와 히로시게]][[kw:Hiroshige]][[la:Hiroshige]][[lt:Hiroshige]][[mg:Hiroshige]][[nl:Ando Hiroshige]][[nn:Hiroshige]][[no:Hiroshige]][[oc:Hiroshige]][[pl:Hiroshige Andō]][[pt:Hiroshige]][[ro:Hiroshige]][[ru:Утагава Хиросигэ]][[sco:Hiroshige]][[sh:Hiroshige]][[simple:Hiroshige]][[sl:Utagava Hirošige]][[sr:Хирошиге]][[sv:Ando Hiroshige]][[th:ฮิโรชิเงะ]][[tr:Utagawa Hiroshige]][[uk:Утаґава Хіросіґе]][[vi:Hiroshige]][[war:Hiroshige]][[wuu:歌川广重]][[xmf:ანდო ჰიროშიგე]][[yo:Hiroshige]][[zh-yue:歌川廣重]][[zh:歌川廣重]][[zu:Hiroshige]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Memorial Portrait of Hiroshige, by Kunisada.jpg|birth_date=1797|death_date={{death date|1858|10|12}}|birth_place={{ill|Edo|qid=Q215646|1=Edo}}|death_place={{ill|Edo|qid=Q215646|1=Edo}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|painter|qid=Q1028181|1=Painter}}; {{ill|ukiyo-e artist|qid=Q33082999|1=ukiyo-e artist}}; {{ill|xylographer|qid=Q1437754|1=xylographer}}|title={{ill|dōshin|qid=Q4357164|1=Dōshin}}|notable_works={{ill|Eight Views of Ōmi|qid=Q340790|1=Eight Views of Ōmi}}; {{ill|Famous Views of the 60-odd Provinces|qid=Q6724086|1=Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces}}; {{ill|Kōshū Nikki|qid=Q11577752|1=Kōshū Nikki}}; {{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}; {{ill|Tendō Hiroshige|qid=Q36967586|1=Tendō Hiroshige}}; {{ill|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|qid=Q3235562|1=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō}}; {{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|qid=Q2525862|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji (Hiroshige)}}|native_name=歌川広重|given_name={{ill|Hiroshige|qid=Q16290297|1=Hiroshige (given name)}}|resting_place={{ill|Tōgaku-ji|qid=Q97203084|1=Tōgaku-ji Temple}}}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2020}} {{short description|Japanese ukiyo-e woodblock print artist}} {{see also|Hiroshige (given name)|Hiroshige (crater)}} {{Infobox artist |name = Hiroshige |native_name = {{nobold|広重}} |image = Memorial Portrait of Utagawa Hiroshige (5765350019).jpg |caption = Memorial portrait of Hiroshige by {{ill|Kunisada|en|Kunisada|qid=Q467427|lt=Kunisada|1=Kunisada}} |alt = |birth_date = {{Birth-date|1797}} |birth_place = {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}, [[Tokugawa shogunate|Japan]] |birth_name = Andō Tokutarō |death_date = {{Death date and age|1858|10|12|1797|df=yes}} |death_place = Edo, Japan |nationality = Japanese |field = {{plainlist| * {{ill|Painting|en|Painting|qid=Q11629|lt=Painting|1=Painting}} * {{ill|Woodblock printing in Japan|en|Woodblock printing in Japan|qid=Q1192525|lt=Printing|1=Woodblock printing in Japan}}}} |training = {{ill|Toyohiro|en|Toyohiro|qid=Q2625013|lt=Toyohiro|1=Toyohiro}} |movement = {{ill|Utagawa school|en|Utagawa school|qid=Q2297646|lt=Utagawa school|1=Utagawa school}} |works = {{plainlist| * ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|qid=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' * ''{{ill|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|en|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|qid=Q3235562|lt=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|1=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō}}'' * ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}''}} }} '''Utagawa Hiroshige''' ({{IPAc-en|ˌ|h|ɪər|əʊ|ˈ|ʃ|iː|ɡ|eɪ}}, also {{IPAc-en|US|ˌ|h|ɪər|ə|ˈ|-}};{{cite web|url=https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/hiroshige|title=Hiroshige|work=[[Collins English Dictionary]]|publisher=[[HarperCollins]]|access-date=9 May 2019}}{{Cite Merriam-Webster|Hiroshige|access-date=9 May 2019}} {{langx|ja|歌川 広重}} {{IPA|ja|ɯtaɡawa çiɾoꜜɕiɡe|}}), born '''Andō Tokutarō''' ({{lang|ja|安藤 徳太郎}}; 1797 – 12 October 1858), was a Japanese ''[[ukiyo-e]]'' artist, considered the last great master of that tradition. Hiroshige is best known for his horizontal-format landscape series ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|qid=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' and for his vertical-format landscape series ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}''. The subjects of his work were atypical of the ''ukiyo-e'' genre, whose typical focus was on beautiful women, popular actors, and other scenes of the urban pleasure districts of Japan's {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603–1868). The popular series ''{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|en|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|qid=Q227494|lt=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji}}'' by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|qid=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}} was a strong influence on Hiroshige's choice of subject, though Hiroshige's approach was more poetic and ambient than Hokusai's bolder, more formal prints. Subtle use of color was essential in Hiroshige's prints, often printed with multiple impressions in the same area and with extensive use of ''{{ill|Bokashi (printing)|en|Bokashi (printing)|qid=Q2909205|lt=bokashi|1=Bokashi (printing)}}'' (color gradation), both of which were rather labor-intensive techniques. For scholars and collectors, Hiroshige's death marked the beginning of a rapid decline in the ''ukiyo-e'' genre, especially in the face of the westernization that followed the [[Meiji Restoration]] of 1868. Hiroshige's work came to have a marked influence on western European painting towards the close of the 19th century as a part of the trend in [[:en:Japonism|Japonism]]. Western European artists, such as [[:en:Manet|Manet]] and [[:en:Monet|Monet]], collected and closely studied Hiroshige's compositions: {{ill|Vincent van Gogh|en|Vincent van Gogh|qid=Q5582|lt=Vincent van Gogh|1=Vincent van Gogh}}, for instance, [[Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)#Copies of Hiroshige prints|painted copies]] of some Hiroshige prints. ==Early life and apprenticeship== [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Wind Blown Grass Across the Moon - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|thumb|left|''Wind Blown Grass Across the Moon'' – by Hiroshige]] [[File:Brooklyn Museum - Returning Sails at Tsukuda from Eight Views of Edo - Utagawa Toyohiro.jpg|thumb|alt=|Hiroshige studied under {{ill|Toyohiro|en|Toyohiro|qid=Q2625013|lt=Toyohiro|1=Toyohiro}} of the {{ill|Utagawa school|en|Utagawa school|qid=Q2297646|lt=Utagawa school|1=Utagawa school}} of artists.
''Returning Sails at Tsukuda'', from ''Eight Views of Edo'', early-19th century]] Hiroshige was born in 1797 in the Yayosu Quay section of the {{ill|Yaesu|en|Yaesu|qid=Q1249041|lt=Yaesu|1=Yaesu}} area in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} (modern {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}).{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=69}} He was of a {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} background,{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=69}} and is the great-grandson of Tanaka Tokuemon, who held a position of power under the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|qid=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} in the northern province of [[:en:Mutsu province|Mutsu]]. Hiroshige's grandfather, Mitsuemon, was an archery instructor who worked under the name Sairyūken. Hiroshige's father, Gen'emon, was {{ill|Japanese adult adoption|en|Japanese adult adoption|qid=Q17050042|lt=adopted into the family|1=Japanese adult adoption}} of Andō Jūemon, whom he succeeded as [[:en:fire warden|fire warden]] for the Yayosu Quay area.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=69}} Hiroshige went through several name changes as a youth: Jūemon, Tokubē, and Tetsuzō.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=69}} He had three sisters, one of whom died when he was three. His mother died in early 1809, and his father followed later in the year, but not before handing his fire warden duties to his twelve-year-old son.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=70}} He was charged with prevention of fires at {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|qid=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}, a duty that left him much leisure time.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=71}} Not long after his parents' deaths, perhaps at around fourteen, Hiroshige—then named Tokutarō— began painting.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=70}}{{Cite book|last=Uspensky|first=Mikhail|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SQb3AAAAQBAJ&q=hiroshige+biography&pg=PT5|title=Hiroshige|date=2014-01-07|publisher=Parkstone International|isbn=978-1-78160-864-7|language=en}} He sought the tutelage of {{ill|Utagawa Toyokuni|en|Utagawa Toyokuni|qid=Q950316|lt=Toyokuni|1=Utagawa Toyokuni}} of the {{ill|Utagawa school|en|Utagawa school|qid=Q2297646|lt=Utagawa school|1=Utagawa school}}, but Toyokuni had too many pupils to make room for him.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=71}} A librarian introduced him instead to {{ill|Toyohiro|en|Toyohiro|qid=Q2625013|lt=Toyohiro|1=Toyohiro}} of the same school.{{sfn|Oka|1992|pp=70–71}} By 1812 Hiroshige was permitted to sign his works, which he did under the {{ill|art name|en|art name|qid=Q39646|lt=art name|1=Art name}} Hiroshige.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=70}} He also studied the techniques of the well-established {{ill|Kanō school|en|Kanō school|qid=Q252801|lt=Kanō school|1=Kanō school}}, the {{ill|Nanga (Japanese painting)|en|Nanga (Japanese painting)|qid=Q2928221|lt=nanga|1=Nanga (Japanese painting)}} whose tradition began with the Chinese {{ill|Southern School|en|Southern School|qid=Q1142000|lt=Southern School|1=Southern School}}, and the realistic {{ill|Shijō school|en|Shijō school|qid=Q3577704|lt=Shijō school|1=Shijō school}}, and likely the {{ill|perspective (graphical)|en|perspective (graphical)|qid=Q1900281|lt=linear perspective|1=Perspective (graphical)}} techniques of Western art and {{ill|uki-e|en|uki-e|qid=Q3547879|lt=uki-e|1=Uki-e}}.{{sfn|Oka|1992|pp=71–72}} Hiroshige's apprentice work included book illustrations and single-sheet ''ukiyo-e'' prints of female beauties and {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|qid=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} actors in the Utagawa style, sometimes signing them Ichiyūsai{{sfn|Oka|1992|pp=72–73}} or, from 1832, Ichiryūsai.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=74}} In 1823, he passed his post as fire warden on to his son,{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hiroshige|title=Hiroshige {{!}} Japanese artist|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en|access-date=2020-04-03}} though he still acted as an alternate.{{efn|Hiroshige's resignation has led to conjecture: nominally, he passed the position to his son Nakajirō, but it may have been that Nakajirō was actually the son of his adoptive grandfather. Hiroshige, as adopted heir, may have been made to give up the position to the purported legitimate heir.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=71}} }} He declined an offer to succeed Toyohiro upon the master's death in 1828.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=71}} ==Landscapes, flora, and fauna== It was not until 1829–1830 that Hiroshige began to produce the landscapes he has come to be known for, such as the ''{{ill|Eight Views of Ōmi|en|Eight Views of Ōmi|qid=Q340790|lt=Eight Views of Ōmi|1=Eight Views of Ōmi}}'' series.{{sfn|Oka|1992|pp=73–74}} He also created an increasing number of bird and flower prints about this time.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=74}} About 1831, his ''[[Ten Famous Places in the Eastern Capital]]'' appeared, and seem to bear the influence of {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|qid=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, whose popular landscape series ''{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|en|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|qid=Q227494|lt=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji}}'' had recently seen publication.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=75}} {{clear}} An invitation to join an official procession to Kyoto in 1832 gave Hiroshige the opportunity to travel along the {{ill|Tōkaidō (road)|en|Tōkaidō (road)|qid=Q963514|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (road)}} route that linked the two capitals. He sketched the scenery along the way, and when he returned to Edo he produced the series ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|qid=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'', which contains some of his best-known prints.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=75}} Hiroshige built on the series' success by following it with others, such as the ''Illustrated Places of Naniwa'' (1834), ''Famous Places of Kyoto'' (1835), another ''{{ill|Eight Views of Ōmi|en|Eight Views of Ōmi|qid=Q340790|lt=Eight Views of Ōmi|1=Eight Views of Ōmi}}'' (1834). As he had never been west of Kyoto, Hiroshige-based his illustrations of Naniwa (modern {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}) and {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} on pictures found in books and paintings.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=79}} Hiroshige11 hakone.jpg|Print 11: ''Hakone'' Hiroshige16 kanbara.jpg|Print 16: ''Kanbara'' Hiroshige, Travellers surprised by sudden rain.jpg|Print 46: ''Rain Shower at {{ill|Shōno-juku|en|Shōno-juku|qid=Q3033358|lt=Shōno|1=Shōno-juku}}'' Hiroshige's first wife helped finance his trips to sketch travel locations, in one instance selling some of her clothing and ornamental combs. She died in October 1838, and Hiroshige remarried to Oyasu,{{efn|When Hiroshige and Oyasu married is not known.{{sfn|Noguchi|1992|p=177}} }} sixteen years his junior, daughter of a farmer named Kaemon from {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|qid=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province|1=Tōtōmi Province}}.{{sfn|Noguchi|1992|p=177}} Around 1838 Hiroshige produced two series entitled ''[[Eight Views of the Edo Environs]]'', each print accompanied by a humorous ''{{ill|Kyōka|en|Kyōka|qid=Q3201017|lt=Kyōka|1=Kyōka}}'' poem. ''[[:en:The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō]]'' saw print between about 1835 and 1842, a joint production with {{ill|Keisai Eisen|en|Keisai Eisen|qid=Q3046492|lt=Keisai Eisen|1=Keisai Eisen}}, of which Hiroshige's share was forty-six of the seventy prints. {{sfn|Oka|1992|p=81}} ''{{ill|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|en|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|qid=Q3235562|lt=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō|1=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō}}'' was issued jointly by Takenouchi and Iseya Rihei''.''{{Cite book |last=Forrer |first=Matthi |title=Hiroshige |publisher=Prestel |year=1991 |isbn=978-3-7913-4540-6 |edition=1st |location=London |pages=21 |language=English}} Hiroshige produced 118 sheets for the ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}''Forbes & Henley (2014). Full series over the last decade of his life, beginning about 1848.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=83}} In ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}'' Hiroshige frequently places large-scale objects, people and animals, or parts of them, in the foreground. This device, derived from Western art, was intended to add depth to the composition. De pruimenboomgaard te Kameido-Rijksmuseum RP-P-1956-743.jpeg|''Edo'', print 30: ''{{ill|Plum Park in Kameido|en|Plum Park in Kameido|qid=Q21127606|lt=The Plum Garden in Kameido|1=Plum Park in Kameido}}'' 100 views edo 063.jpg|''Edo'', print 63: ''Suidō Bridge and the Surugadai Quarter'' 03 - Sukiyagahsi.jpg|''Thirty-six Views'', print 3: ''Sukiyagashi in the Eastern Capital'' Hiroshige, Futamigaura in Ise Province, 1858.jpg|''Thirty-six Views'', print 27: ''Futami Bay in Ise Province'' {{clear}} [[File:Utagawa Hiroshige (1857) Awa Naruto no fuukei.jpg|thumb|center|500px|alt=|''View of the Whirlpools at Awa'' triptych, 1857, part of the series "[[:en:Snow Moon Flowers|Snow, Moon and Flowers]]"]] ===Hiroshige's students=== {{ill|Hiroshige II|en|Hiroshige II|qid=Q653454|lt=Hiroshige II|1=Hiroshige II}} was a young print artist, Chinpei Suzuki, who married Hiroshige's daughter, Otatsu. He was given the artist name of "Shigenobu". Hiroshige intended to make Shigenobu his heir in all matters, and Shigenobu adopted the name "Hiroshige" after his master's death in 1858, and thus today is known as Hiroshige II. However, the marriage to Otatsu was troubled and in 1865 they separated. Otatsu was remarried to another former pupil of Hiroshige, Shigemasa, who appropriated the name of the lineage and today is known as {{ill|Hiroshige III|en|Hiroshige III|qid=Q653932|lt=Hiroshige III|1=Hiroshige III}}. Both Hiroshige II and Hiroshige III worked in a distinctive style based on that of Hiroshige, but neither achieved the level of success and recognition accorded to their master. Other students of Hiroshige I include [[Utagawa Shigemaru]], [[Utagawa Shigekiyo]], and {{ill|Utagawa Hirokage|en|Utagawa Hirokage|qid=Q3552725|lt=Utagawa Hirokage|1=Utagawa Hirokage}}. Hiroshige II Suō Iwakuni.jpg|''Suō Iwakuni'', {{ill|Hiroshige II|en|Hiroshige II|qid=Q653454|lt=Hiroshige II|1=Hiroshige II}}, 1859 Hiroshige III - Teppōzu Akashi-bashi.jpg|''Teppōzu Akashi-bashi'', {{ill|Hiroshige III|en|Hiroshige III|qid=Q653932|lt=Hiroshige III|1=Hiroshige III}}, {{circa|1870}} Hirokage - Comic Incidents at Famous Places in Edo (Edo meisho dôke zukushi), No. 22, dog stealing a workman's meal from a snow Daruma.jpg|''Dog stealing a workman's meal from a snow [[Daruma doll|Daruma]]'', {{ill|Utagawa Hirokage|en|Utagawa Hirokage|qid=Q3552725|lt=Hirokage|1=Utagawa Hirokage}}, {{circa|1855–56}} ==Late life== [[File:牛込神楽坂の図 歌川広重 1840.jpg|thumb|View of {{ill|Kagurazaka|en|Kagurazaka|qid=Q3275957|lt=Kagurazaka|1=Kagurazaka}} and {{ill|Ushigome|en|Ushigome|qid=Q7901941|lt=Ushigome|1=Ushigome}} bridge to {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|qid=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} (牛込神楽坂の図), by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]], 1840]] In his declining years, Hiroshige still produced thousands of prints to meet the demand for his works, but few were as good as those of his early and middle periods. He never lived in financial comfort, even in old age. In no small part, his prolific output stemmed from the fact that he was poorly paid per series, although he was still capable of remarkable art when the conditions were right — his great ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}'' ({{lang|ja|名所江戸百景}} ''Meisho Edo Hyakkei'') was paid for up-front by a wealthy {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} priest in love with the daughter of the publisher, Uoya Eikichi (a former fishmonger). In 1856, Hiroshige "retired from the world," becoming a Buddhist monk; this was the year he began his ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo''. He died aged 62 during the great Edo {{ill|Cholera|en|Cholera|qid=Q12090|lt=Cholera|1=Cholera}} epidemic of 1858 (whether the epidemic killed him is unknown) and was buried in a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen Buddhist|1=Zen}} temple in [[Asakusa]]. Just before his death, he left a farewell poem: {{Quote |text= {{lang|ja|東路に}} {{lang|ja|筆を残して}} {{lang|ja|旅の空}} {{lang|ja|西のみくにの}} {{lang|ja|名所を見む}} I leave my brush in the East, And set forth on my journey. I shall see the famous places in the Western Land. }} (The Western Land in this context refers to the strip of land by the Tōkaidō between Kyoto and Edo, but it does double duty as a reference to the paradise of the [[:en:Amida Buddha|Amida Buddha]]). Despite his productivity and popularity, Hiroshige was not wealthy—his commissions were less than those of other in-demand artists, amounting to an income of about twice the wages of a day labourer. His will left instructions for the payment of his debts.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=68}} ==Works== [[Image:Hiroshige Man on horseback crossing a bridge.jpg|thumb|left|300px|A rather dark printing of the view sometimes dubbed "Man on Horseback Crossing a Bridge." From the series ''The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō'', this is View 28 and Station 27 at {{ill|Nagakubo-shuku|en|Nagakubo-shuku|qid=Q3335055|lt=Nagakubo-shuku|1=Nagakubo-shuku}}, depicting the Wada Bridge across the Yoda River.{{cite web |url=http://www.hiroshige.org.uk/hiroshige/kisokaido/kisokaido03.htm |title=Kisokaido Road |publisher=Hiroshige |access-date=26 December 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111213103847/http://www.hiroshige.org.uk/hiroshige/kisokaido/kisokaido03.htm |archive-date=13 December 2011 }} ]] Hiroshige produced over 8,000 works.{{sfn|Oka|1992|pp=67–68}} He largely confined himself in his early work to common ''ukiyo-e'' themes such as women ({{lang|ja|美人画}} {{transl|ja|bijin-ga}}) and actors ({{lang|ja|役者絵}} {{transl|ja|yakusha-e}}). Then, after the death of Toyohiro, Hiroshige made a dramatic turnabout, with the 1831 landscape series ''Famous Views of the Eastern Capital'' ({{lang|ja|東都名所}} {{transl|ja|Tōto Meisho}}) which was critically acclaimed for its composition and colors. This set is generally distinguished from Hiroshige's many print sets depicting Edo by referring to it as ''Ichiyūsai Gakki'', a title derived from the fact that he signed it as Ichiyūsai Hiroshige. With ''{{ill|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|en|The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|qid=Q243248|lt=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō|1=The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō}}'' (1833–1834), his success was assured. These designs were drawn from Hiroshige's actual travels of the full distance of {{convert|490|km|mi|sp=us}}. They included details of date, location, and anecdotes of his fellow travelers, and were immensely popular. In fact, this series was so popular that he reissued it in three versions, one of which was made jointly with Kunisada.Christine Guth, Art of Edo Japan: The Artist and the City, 1615–1868 (Harry Abrams, 1996). {{ISBN|0-8109-2730-6}} Hiroshige went on to produce more than 2000 different prints of Edo and [[shukuba|post stations]] Tōkaidō, as well as series such as ''[[:en:The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō]]'' (1834–1842) and his own ''{{ill|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji (Hiroshige)|en|Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji (Hiroshige)|qid=Q2525862|lt=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji|1=Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji (Hiroshige)}}'' (1852–1858). Of his estimated total of 5000 designs, these landscapes comprised the largest proportion of any genre. He dominated landscape printmaking with his unique brand of intimate, almost small-scale works compared against the older traditions of landscape painting descended from Chinese landscape painters such as [[:en:Sesshu|Sesshu]]. The travel prints generally depict travelers along famous routes experiencing the special attractions of various stops along the way. They travel in the rain, in snow, and during all of the seasons. In 1856, working with the publisher [[Uoya Eikichi]], he created a series of luxury edition prints, made with the finest printing techniques including true gradation of color, the addition of {{ill|mica|en|mica|qid=Q114675|lt=mica|1=Mica}} to lend a unique iridescent effect, embossing, fabric printing, blind printing, and the use of glue printing (wherein ink is mixed with glue for a glittery effect). Hiroshige pioneered the use of the vertical format in landscape printing in his series {{ill|Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|en|Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|qid=Q6724086|lt=Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|1=Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces}}. ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' (issued serially between 1856 and 1859) was immensely popular. The set was published posthumously and some prints had not been completed — he had created over 100 on his own, but two were added by Hiroshige II after his death. ==Influence== [[File:Kisokaido20 Oiwake.jpg|thumb|alt=|{{ill|Keisai Eisen|en|Keisai Eisen|qid=Q3046492|lt=Keisai Eisen|1=Keisai Eisen}} was influenced by and worked with Hiroshige.
''Oiwake'', from ''{{ill|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|en|The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|qid=Q3235562|lt=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō|1=The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kiso Kaidō}}'', 1830s]] Hiroshige was a member of the {{ill|Utagawa school|en|Utagawa school|qid=Q2297646|lt=Utagawa school|1=Utagawa school}}, along with {{ill|Kunisada|en|Kunisada|qid=Q467427|lt=Kunisada|1=Kunisada}} and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi|Kuniyoshi]]. The Utagawa school comprised dozens of artists, and stood at the forefront of 19th century woodblock prints. Particularly noteworthy for their actor and historical prints, members of the Utagawa school were nonetheless well-versed in all of the popular genres. During Hiroshige's time, the print industry was booming, and the consumer audience for prints was growing rapidly. Prior to this time, most print series had been issued in small sets, such as ten or twelve designs per series. Increasingly large series were produced to meet demand, and this trend can be seen in Hiroshige's work, such as ''The Sixty-nine Stations of the Kisokaidō'' and ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo''. In terms of style, Hiroshige is especially noted for using unusual vantage points, seasonal allusions, and striking colors. In particular, he worked extensively within the realm of ''{{transl|ja|meisho-e}}'' ({{lang|ja|名所絵}}) pictures of famous places. During the Edo period, tourism was also booming, leading to increased popular interest in travel. Travel guides abounded, and towns appeared along routes such as the Tōkaidō, a road that connected Edo with Kyoto. In the midst of this burgeoning travel culture, Hiroshige drew upon his own travels, as well as tales of others' adventures, for inspiration in creating his landscapes. For example, in ''The Fifty-three Stations on the Tōkaidō'' (1833), he illustrates anecdotes from ''Travels on the Eastern Seaboard'' ({{lang|ja|東海道中膝栗毛}} {{transl|ja|Tōkaidōchū Hizakurige}}, 1802–1809) by {{ill|Jippensha Ikku|en|Jippensha Ikku|qid=Q260290|lt=Jippensha Ikku|1=Jippensha Ikku}}, a comedy describing the adventures of two bumbling travelers as they make their way along the same road. Hiroshige's ''The Fifty-three Stations of the Tōkaidō'' (1833–1834) and ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' (1856–1858) greatly influenced {{ill|France|en|France|qid=Q142|lt=French|1=France}} [[:en:Impressionists|Impressionists]] such as [[:en:Monet|Monet]]. {{ill|Vincent van Gogh|en|Vincent van Gogh|qid=Q5582|lt=Vincent van Gogh|1=Vincent van Gogh}} copied two of the ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' which were among his collection of ukiyo-e prints. Hiroshige's style also influenced the {{ill|Mir iskusstva|en|Mir iskusstva|qid=Q1425138|lt=Mir iskusstva|1=Mir iskusstva}}, a 20th-century {{ill|Russia|en|Russia|qid=Q159|lt=Russia|1=Russia}}n art movement in which {{ill|Ivan Bilibin|en|Ivan Bilibin|qid=Q312024|lt=Ivan Bilibin|1=Ivan Bilibin}} and {{ill|Mstislav Dobuzhinsky|en|Mstislav Dobuzhinsky|qid=Q972018|lt=Mstislav Dobuzhinsky|1=Mstislav Dobuzhinsky}} were major artists. Dobuzhinsky confessed of Hiroshige's influence "I liked to choose a viewpoint of my own so that the composition would be striking, unusual; in that, I had the constant example of Hiroshige before my eyes".{{Cite book|last1=Mikhailova|first1=Yulia|title=Japan and Russia: Three Centuries of Mutual Images|url=https://archive.org/details/japanrussiathree00mikh|url-access=limited|last2=William Steele|first2=M.|publisher=Global Oriental Ltd|year=2008|isbn=978-1905246427|location=Folkestone, Kent|pages=[https://archive.org/details/japanrussiathree00mikh/page/n50 37], 41}} {{ill|Paul Cézanne|en|Paul Cézanne|qid=Q35548|lt=Cézanne|1=Paul Cézanne}} and [[:en:James Abbott McNeill Whistler|Whistler]] were also amongst those under Hiroshige's influence.{{sfn|Oka|1992|p=67}} Hiroshige was regarded by Louise Gonse, director of the influential ''{{ill|Gazette des Beaux-Arts|en|Gazette des Beaux-Arts|qid=Q1287286|lt=Gazette des Beaux-Arts|1=Gazette des Beaux-Arts}}'' and author of the two volume ''L'Art Japonais'' in 1883, as the greatest painter of landscapes of the 19th century.{{cite book |author1=G.P. Weisberg |author2=P.D. Cate |author3=G. Needham |author4=M. Eidelberg |author5=W.R. Johnston |title=Japonisme – Japanese Influence on French Art 1854–1910 |year=1975 |location=London |publisher=[[Cleveland Museum of Art]], [[Walters Art Gallery]], Robert G. Sawyers Publications |isbn=0-910386-22-6}} Hiroshige Atake sous une averse soudaine.jpg|''{{ill|Sudden Shower over Shin-Ōhashi Bridge and Atake|en|Sudden Shower over Shin-Ōhashi Bridge and Atake|qid=Q5826309|lt=Sudden Shower Over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake|1=Sudden Shower over Shin-Ōhashi Bridge and Atake}}'', Hiroshige, 1857
One of the ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}'' Vincent van Gogh - Brug in de regen- naar Hiroshige - Google Art Project.jpg|''Bridge in the rain (after Hiroshige)'', {{ill|Vincent van Gogh|en|Vincent van Gogh|qid=Q5582|lt=Vincent van Gogh|1=Vincent van Gogh}} (from ''{{ill|Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)|en|Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)|qid=Q6159323|lt=Japonaiserie|1=Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)}}''), oil on canvas, 1887 De pruimenboomgaard te Kameido-Rijksmuseum RP-P-1956-743.jpeg|''{{ill|Plum Park in Kameido|en|Plum Park in Kameido|qid=Q21127606|lt=The Plum Garden in Kameido|1=Plum Park in Kameido}}'' (1857), from Hiroshige's ''{{ill|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|en|One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|qid=Q165190|lt=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo|1=One Hundred Famous Views of Edo}}'' Vincent van Gogh - Bloeiende pruimenboomgaard- naar Hiroshige - Google Art Project.jpg|''Flowering Plum Tree (after Hiroshige)'' (1887) by {{ill|Vincent van Gogh|en|Vincent van Gogh|qid=Q5582|lt=Vincent van Gogh|1=Vincent van Gogh}}, from his ''{{ill|Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)|en|Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)|qid=Q6159323|lt=Japonaiserie|1=Japonaiserie (Van Gogh)}}'', in the collection of the {{ill|Van Gogh Museum|en|Van Gogh Museum|qid=Q224124|lt=Van Gogh Museum|1=Van Gogh Museum}} in Amsterdam ==Gallery== File:The Famous Scenes of the Sixty States 55 Awa.jpg|Naruto Whirlpool, Awa Province, from ''{{ill|Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|en|Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|qid=Q6724086|lt=Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces|1=Famous Views of the Sixty-odd Provinces}}'' File:Brooklyn Museum - Moonlight View of Tsukuda with Lady on a Balcony - Utagawa Hiroshige (Ando).jpg|Moonlight View of Tsukuda with Lady on a Balcony File:Sumida River, the Wood of the Water God, at Masaki LACMA M.66.35.5.jpg|Sumida River, the Wood of the Water god File:Ando Hiroshige - Moon over Ships Moored at Tsukuda Island from Eitai Bridge - Google Art Project.jpg|Moon over Ships Moored at Tsukuda Island from Eitai Bridge File:Utagawa Hiroshige - Evening on the Sumida river - Google Art Project.jpg|Evening on the Sumida river File:Utagawa Hiroshige - Enjoying the fireworks and the cool of the evening at Ryogoku bridge in the Eastern Capital - Google Art Project.jpg|Enjoying the fireworks and the cool of the evening at Ryogoku bridge File:100 views edo 111.jpg|Moon Bridge in Meguro, from ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' File:Ando Hiroshige - Horikiri Iris Garden (Horikiri no hanashōbu), from the series One Hundred Famous Views of Edo (Meish... - Google Art Project.jpg|Horikiri Iris Garden (Horikiri no hanashōbu), from ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' File:100 views edo 047.jpg|Fudo Falls, Oji, 1857, from ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' File:100 views edo 036.jpg|View from Massaki of Suijin Shrine, Uchigawa Inlet, and Sekiya, from ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' File:100 views edo 045.jpg|Yoroi Ferry, Koami-cho, from ''One Hundred Famous Views of Edo'' File:Dragon in clouds by Utagawa Hiroshige.jpg|''Dragon in clouds'' File:Hiroshige Heavy rain on a pine tree 2.jpg|Heavy rain on a pine tree, from ''{{ill|Eight Views of Ōmi|en|Eight Views of Ōmi|qid=Q340790|lt=Eight Views of Ōmi|1=Eight Views of Ōmi}}'' File:Hiroshige Fishing boats on a lake.jpg|Fishing boats on a lake, from ''Eight Views of Ōmi'' File:Hiroshige Full moon over a mountain landscape.jpg|Full moon over a mountain landscape, from ''Eight Views of Ōmi'' File:Sokokura by Hiroshige1.jpg|Sokokura, from ''Seven Hot Springs of Hakone'' File:Hiroshige View of a long bridge across a lake.jpg| View of a long bridge across a lake, from ''Eight Views of Ōmi'' File:Hiroshige A shrine among trees on a moor.jpg|A shrine among trees on a moor ==See also== *{{ill|List of Utagawa school members|en|List of Utagawa school members|qid=Q1116063|lt=List of Utagawa school members|1=List of Utagawa school members}} * ==Notes== {{Notelist}} ===Notes=== {{Reflist|colwidth=30em}} ===References=== {{Refbegin}} * Forbes, Andrew; Henley, David (2014).
